hero.tagline: Hawaii's small claims court offers a streamlined process with a $5,000 cap and no right to appeal.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Permitted but not required; restricted in security deposit disputes

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Decisions are final with no right to appeal

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $5,000 cap, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the limit. Instead, you must file in the regular civil division of the District Court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii allows you to file claims up to $5,000 for disputes involving contracts and money owed. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $3,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness testimony.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $2,500 to a colleague who promised to repay in 6 months but hasn't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Gather any written agreements, payment records, and communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $4,500 and missed the repayment deadline.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and any records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $3,000 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,800 on their store account.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent over several months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $2,000 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the bounced check, bank notice, and any related communication.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $5,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the loan agreement and records of payments you've made.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $5,000, including issues with products and services. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 appliance that stopped working shortly after purchase, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from date of delivery. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $800 but did a poor job, and they refuse to refund or redo the work.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any expert opinions.

claims.name: Incomplete or poor-quality contractor work
claims.example: A contractor was paid $4,000 to renovate your bathroom but left the job unfinished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods or services
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for catering services, but the vendor never showed up.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month subscription, but the company continued to charge your account.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale, any warranties, and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A retailer advertised a product for $500 with features it didn't have, leading you to purchase under false pretenses.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from discovery. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and any correspondence.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii's small claims court addresses property damage claims up to $5,000. The statute of limitations is generally 2 years from the date of damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,500 in damages, and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,000 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $4,500 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and damaged $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property while in someone else's custody
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and any communication with the shop.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii's small claims court handles various landlord-tenant disputes, including security deposit issues and unpaid rent. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without justification after you moved out.
claims.notes: No monetary limit for security deposit disputes. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: No monetary limit for security deposit disputes. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your rental unit had persistent mold issues that the landlord refused to address.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide photos, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, the landlord kept your furniture valued at $3,000.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, inventory of items, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your rental.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement, photos of the changed locks, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent after termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after you moved out early.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent over several months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $1,200 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord refused, and deducted it from your rent.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide repair receipts, photos, and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii's small claims court addresses wage disputes up to $5,000. The statute of limitations is generally 6 years from the date wages were due.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $4,000 in unpaid wages for hours worked.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from due date. Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to provide final paycheck
claims.example: After leaving your job, your employer didn't issue your final paycheck of $2,500.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from due date. Provide resignation letter, final timesheet, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from due date. Bring sales records, commission agreements, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: Your employer didn't pay the required overtime rate for 50 hours of overtime worked.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from due date. Provide timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii's small claims court handles personal claims up to $5,000, including minor injuries and property disputes. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell at a store, incurring $3,500 in medical expenses.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Bring medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,200 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from demand for return. Provide proof of ownership, any loan agreements, and communication with the borrower.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $2,500 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from theft date. Bring proof of ownership, police reports, and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Hawaii's small claims court addresses vehicle-related disputes up to $5,000. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any expert opinions.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you incurred $1,200 in fees.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from tow date. Provide towing receipts, photos of the parking spot, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court does not handle eviction proceedings; these are addressed in the regular civil division of the District Court.
Disputes involving ownership or title to real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation, libel, or slander are not handled in small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limits of small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are not within the purview of small claims court.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are handled by the probate court, not small claims.
Small claims court does not have jurisdiction over cases against federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot grant orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
Disputes over workers' compensation are handled by the Department of Labor and Industrial Relations.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Department of Labor and Industrial Relations

exclusions.explanation: Bankruptcy cases are under federal jurisdiction and not handled by small claims court.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not within the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not permitted in Hawaii's small claims court; only actual damages can be awarded.
damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees are generally not awarded in small claims court, except in specific cases where statutes provide for fee shifting.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the District Court

filing_options: Judicial circuit where the defendant resides or where the claim arose
Judicial circuit where the rental property is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: As of April 2026, e-filing is not available for small claims cases in Hawaii.

service_fees.notes: Cost depends on postal rates; typically between $5 and $15.

service_fees.method: Personal service by sheriff or process server
service_fees.notes: Fees vary by provider; typically between $25 and $50.

forms.name: Application to Proceed In Forma Pauperis

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (e.g., SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% of the federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees upon court approval.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

other_fees.name: Writ of execution
other_fees.notes: Fee for issuing a writ to enforce a judgment.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

entries.notes: Applies to contracts not in writing.
Applies to contracts for the sale of goods.
Applies to written promises to pay money.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

entries.notes: Applies to claims for bodily harm.
Applies to damage to personal or real property.
Applies to checks returned for insufficient funds.
Applies to claims for earned but unpaid wages.
Applies to claims for return of security deposits.
Applies to actions to enforce court judgments.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, it's advisable to send a written demand for payment before filing a lawsuit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: For unpaid wage claims, providing written notice to the employer can strengthen your case.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Tenants should provide a forwarding address to landlords to facilitate the return of security deposits.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: For consumer protection claims, documenting all communications and attempts to resolve the issue is beneficial.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the relief sought.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and the date of the hearing.

forms.name: Proof of Service
forms.description: Documents the method and completion of serving the defendant.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Allows the defendant to file a claim against the plaintiff within the same case.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Application to Proceed In Forma Pauperis
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Certified or registered mail
methods.description: The court clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified or registered mail with return receipt requested.
cons: Defendant may refuse to accept mail

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff or process server
methods.description: A sheriff or licensed process server personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent of the corporation or, if none, an officer or managing agent. For sole proprietors, serve the individual owner.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Hawaii rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is served.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located after diligent efforts, the court may allow service by publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document all attempts and consider requesting alternative service methods from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer; they may appear at the hearing to present their defense.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 10 days of the judgment; if denied, the decision is final as there is no right to appeal in small claims court.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit but is within the jurisdictional limit of the District Court, the case remains in small claims court unless both parties agree to transfer.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are permitted but not required; however, in security deposit disputes, attorneys cannot represent either party unless they are the landlord or tenant themselves.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial where both parties present their cases directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk as soon as possible before the hearing date.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts may offer mediation services on the day of the hearing to help parties reach a settlement.

appeals.type_notes: Decisions in small claims court are final and cannot be appealed.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: Default judgments cannot be appealed; the appropriate remedy is to file a motion to set aside the default judgment.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at a rate of 10% per annum.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns sufficient wages.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is greater

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the bank account.
exemptions: $1,000 in the debtor's account is exempt from levy

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment amount.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the Bureau of Conveyances to create a lien on the debtor's real property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize non-exempt property, and sell it at public auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.
exemptions: Personal property up to $1,000 in value

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year period expires; if granted, the judgment is extended for another 10 years.

debtor_exam_form.name: Motion for Examination of Judgment Debtor

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized based on the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.

non_attorney_representation_form.name: Authorization to Appear on Behalf of Business Entity

businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Certificate of Assumed Name with the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors can authorize an employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from suing to recover fees for work requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs records.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Hawaii's rules for service of process carefully, using certified mail or a qualified process server.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather and organize all relevant documents, photos, and witness information before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court deadlines
pitfalls.what_happens: Missing deadlines for filings or appearances can result in default judgments or case dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Keep track of all court dates and deadlines, and ensure timely submissions and appearances.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking collection procedures
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; failure to follow proper collection procedures can leave you unpaid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with Hawaii's judgment collection methods and take appropriate steps to enforce the judgment.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Hawaii's small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves. However, you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases in Hawaii are typically scheduled for a hearing within 30 days of filing. The duration of the case can vary depending on factors such as service of process and court schedules.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Hawaii's small claims court, you can sue for up to $5,000, exclusive of interest and costs. There is no monetary limit for cases involving the return of a residential security deposit.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the hearing after being properly served, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor. You will still need to provide evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: No, decisions made in Hawaii's small claims court are final and cannot be appealed. However, the court may alter or set aside a judgment under certain circumstances.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: In Hawaii, a judgment is valid for 10 years from the date it is entered. You can renew the judgment before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Hawaii's small claims court has exclusive jurisdiction over residential security deposit disputes, with no monetary limit on these cases.
Decisions in Hawaii's small claims court are final and cannot be appealed, emphasizing the importance of thorough preparation.
Attorneys are generally allowed in small claims court, but in security deposit disputes, they cannot represent either party unless they are the landlord or tenant themselves.
The filing fee for small claims cases in Hawaii is a flat $35, making it accessible for individuals seeking to resolve disputes.
Hawaii's small claims court does not handle eviction proceedings; these are addressed in the regular civil division of the District Court.

interesting_findings: Hawaii's small claims court allows for the recovery of leased or rented personal property valued at less than $5,000, providing a venue for such disputes.
The court may grant equitable relief in landlord-tenant disputes, including orders to repair, replace, refund, reform, and rescind.
Class actions are prohibited in Hawaii's small claims court, ensuring that cases remain simple and manageable.
The court's jurisdiction includes cases for the return of items stolen from business property, such as shopping carts or baskets.
Hawaii's small claims court emphasizes informal proceedings, allowing individuals to represent themselves effectively.

sources.label: Haw. Rev. Stat. § 480-2 (Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices, UDAP)
Haw. Rev. Stat. § 633-30 (Counterclaim; Retention of Jurisdiction)
